title: Rescattering of non-minimal coupling scalar particles during inflation
abstract: We investigate the rescattering effects arising from non-minimally coupled scalar particles $χ$ that are suddenly produced during inflation. The coupling term $ξR χ^2$ significantly enhances resonant particle production compared to minimal coupling scenarios. Consequently, the produced $χ$ particles rescattering off the homogeneous inflaton condensate $φ$, generating abundant $δφ$ quanta within very short time intervals. This process leads to characteristic enhancements in the power spectrum of primordial curvature perturbations at scales corresponding to the moments of particle production. When this occurs at small scales, the power spectrum amplitude can reach as high as $\mathcal{O}(10^{-2})$. Furthermore, analysis of the equilateral bispectrum shows that this mechanism also induces substantial non-Gaussian features.
extracted_text:
Rescattering of non-minimal coupling scalar particles during
inflation
Zhe Yu,1, ∗Xunliang Yang,2, 3, 4, † and Yungui Gong
1, ‡
1Institute of Fundamental Physics and Quantum Technology,
Department of Physics, School of Physical Science and Technology,
Ningbo University, Ningbo, Zhejiang 315211, China
2School of Fundamental Physics and Mathematical Sciences,
Hangzhou Institute for Advanced Study, UCAS, Hangzhou 310024, China
3Institute of Theoretical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences,Beijing 100190,China
4University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China
We investigate the rescattering effects arising from non-minimally coupled scalar
particles χ that are suddenly produced during inflation. The coupling term ξRχ2
significantly enhances resonant particle production compared to minimal coupling
scenarios. Consequently, the produced χ particles rescattering off the homogeneous
inflaton condensate ϕ, generating abundant δϕ quanta within very short time inter-
vals. This process leads to characteristic enhancements in the power spectrum of
primordial curvature perturbations at scales corresponding to the moments of par-
ticle production. When this occurs at small scales, the power spectrum amplitude
can reach as high as O(10−2). Furthermore, analysis of the equilateral bispectrum
shows that this mechanism also induces substantial non-Gaussian features.
I.
INTRODUCTION
The inflationary paradigm [1–4] resolves fundamental issues of the standard cosmological
model and has become the prevailing framework for describing the early universe. During
inflation, the accelerated expansion causes the comoving Hubble radius to shrink exponen-
tially, stretching quantum vacuum fluctuations beyond the horizon where they freeze [5–7].
Among these perturbations, the primordial curvature perturbation which is associated with
∗yuzhe@nbu.edu.cn
† yangxunliang22@mails.ucas.ac.cn
‡ gongyungui@nbu.edu.cn
arXiv:2508.07381v1  [astro-ph.CO]  10 Aug 2025


2
the scalar modes, dominates the observed anisotropies in the cosmic microwave background
(CMB). Precision measurements from contemporary CMB experiments such as Planck [8, 9],
BICEP/Keck [10, 11], and the latest ACT results [12, 13] have tightly constrained the cur-
vature perturbations. These datasets determine the amplitude of the power spectrum at
the pivot scale k = 0.05Mpc−1 to be approximately 2.1 × 10−9 with slight scale dependence
[8], constrain the scalar spectral index to ns = 0.974 ± 0.03 through joint analyses [12], and
set an upper limit on the tensor-to-scalar ratio of r < 0.036 (95% CL), thereby limiting
the presence of primordial gravitational waves (GWs) [11]. While these observations pre-
cisely characterize curvature perturbations on large (CMB) scales, their properties on small
scales remain largely unconstrained due to observational limitations. This gap motivates
extensive theoretical investigations into mechanisms that can modify primordial curvature
perturbations from standard slow-roll expectations at these small scales [14–17]. In this
work, we explore how rescattering of non-minimally coupled scalar particles leads to correc-
tions in the power spectrum of primordial curvature perturbations and induces distinctive
equilateral-type bispectra.
Scalar particle production during inflation has been widely studied [18–21], with partic-
ular attention to burst-like scenarios characterized by rapid particle generation [22–27]. In
these scenarios, an additional scalar field χ is coupled to the inflaton ϕ through an interac-
tion of the form g2(ϕ −ϕ0)2χ2. When the inflaton ϕ approaches the value ϕ0, the system
experiences a non-adiabatic transition, violating the adiabatic condition ˙ωk ≤ω2
k, where
ω2
k ≈g2(ϕ −ϕ0)2 characterizes the frequency of mode χk. Efficient particle production re-
quires the nonadiabatic time scale, ∆t = 1/(−g ˙ϕ0)
1
2 to be shorter than a Hubble time. After
the burst, the effective mass of χ grows quickly, preventing condensate formation. Because
occupation numbers satisfy nk < 1 for all modes, this minimal model yields inefficient GW
production. To boost particle production efficiency, non-minimal couplings to gravity are
introduced [28–32]. Specifically, the interaction ξRχ2 induces a tachyonic instability near
ϕ ≈ϕ0, with the effective mass-squared given by m2
eff = g2(ϕ −ϕ0)2 −ξR. When this
quantity becomes negative, χ-particle are exponentially amplified. However, the large oc-
cupation numbers that result lead to significant backreaction on the inflaton dynamics [30],
which quenches further particle production. Although GW signals in this non-minimal sce-
nario are stronger than in the minimal model, they remain below the sensitivity of detectors
such LISA[33, 34], TianQin [35, 36], and Taiji [37], due to rapid mass acquisition by χ


3
particles. Consequently, signatures of particle production imprinted on curvature perturba-
tions become critical observational targets. In our recent work [38], employing the Hartree
approximation, we incorporated backreaction effects from strongly produced particles via
non-minimal coupling in the Starobinsky inflation model, demonstrating an enhancement
in the scalar spectral index ns within two e-folds consistent at the 1σ level with the current
P+ACT+LB+BK18 constraints.
Going beyond the Hartree approximation, rescattering processes constitute the leading-
order source of backreaction: rapidly produced χ-particles interact nonlinearly with the
inflaton background, transferring momentum to the inflaton fluctuations δϕ. These rescat-
tering effects following sudden particle production were first analyzed by Barnaby et al. [39],
who treated the produced particles as a classical source term in the equation of motion for
δϕ, deriving enhanced curvature perturbations. However, their classical treatment omitted
important leading-order quantum contributions. Pearce et al. [40] revisited this problem us-
ing the in-in formalism, showing that the correction to the power spectrum is subdominant,
with ∆Pζ/Pζ ≲10−2. More recently, Fumagalli et al. [41] demonstrated that rescatter-
ing of amplified curvature perturbations generates dominant one-loop corrections to the
power spectrum. Analogously, in scenarios with non-minimally coupled particle production,
rescattering can amplify the power spectrum up to O(10−2) on small scales and produce
significant non-Gaussianities characterized by fNL ∼O(1000). Such effects could provide
seeds for primordial black holes (PBHs) and scalar-induced gravitational waves (SIGWs).
This paper is organized as follows. In Section II, we present the non-minimal coupling
model describing sudden particle production during inflation and analyze its rescattering
imprints on curvature perturbations. Subsection II A reviews the particle production mech-
anism using the Bogoliubov coefficient formalism. Subsection II B derives the leading-order
corrections to the curvature power spectrum including rescattering via the in-in formalism.
Subsection II C investigates the induced non-Gaussianities by computing the equilateral
bispectrum and evaluating the fNL parameter over relevant scales. We conclude with a dis-
cussion in Section III. Throughout, we adopt natural units c = ℏ= 1 and set the reduced
Planck mass as Mp ≡(8πG)−1/2 = 1.


4

THE RESCATTERING EFFECTS

From the expression for the occupation number (13), we see that as the parameter ξ grows

on the background dynamics to constrain ξ. Instead of solving the full coupled equations,

B.
Rescattering
In this subsection, we investigate the primordial curvature perturbation by incorporating
the effect of leading-order rescattering of the particle χ within the framework of the in-in
formalism. In this approach, the expectation value of an operator Q at time t is given by

Therefore, the Hubble parameter H can be constrained by the observed amplitude of the
power spectrum at the pivot scale k = 0.05Mpc−1, together with the tensor-to-scalar ratio r
measured at the same scale, resulting in an upper bound H ≤2 × 10−5. Despite potentially

To estimate the magnitude of δ(2)

scale modes during inflation, H = 10−7 and ϵ0 = 10−4. Applying the particle production
duration constraint from Eq. (8), the maximum δ(2)
δϕ is found to be of order 1.63 × 1012 at a
coupling g = 0.00204. This large value indicates that there exists a viable parameter space
where the power spectrum can be sufficiently enhanced to induce PBHs formation and gener-
ate accompany SIGWs. Choosing a larger coupling g = 0.1 results in γ ≈0.01267, consistent
with the duration constraint, and yields δ(2)
δϕ ≈1.68 × 107. Under the same parameters, the
contribution from δ(1)
δϕ is much smaller, approximately 15.6, and thus negligible compared
to δ(2)
δϕ . Consequently, when combined with the unperturbed small-scale power spectrum

III.
CONCLUSION AND DISCUSSION
In this work, we have investigated rescattering processes resulting from sudden particle
production with non-minimal coupling during inflation. The tachyonic instability inherent
in this scenario produces particle numbers vastly exceeding those arising in minimal coupling
cases, leading to notably strong rescattering effects. By incorporating the backreaction of
produced particles on the background dynamics, we computed curvature perturbation power
spectra including one-loop rescattering corrections. Our results demonstrate that the power
spectrum Pζ can be enhanced up to O(10−2) in suitable parameter regimes near the scales
of particle production. Such an amplitude is sufficient to generate both PBHs and SIGWs.
For the same parameter sets that enhance the power spectra, we also quantified the non-
Gaussianity via the nonlinearity parameter fNL, finding enhancements reaching O(103) at
particle production scales. These large values of fNL are characteristic of the model and may
further facilitate PBH formation.


17
Several limitations of our study merit attention: (1) The analytical approximations used
may lead to overestimation of the effects; (2) The shape functions g1(x) and g2(x) do not
include slow-roll corrections, limiting their applicability for spectral index computations at
CMB scales; (3) Backreaction has been treated only to lowest order, thus providing upper
bounds on the parameterξ; and (4) inclusion of the interaction term (23) could modify ⟨χ2⟩,
and consequently affect the final power spectra.
Future work will employ lattice simulations [44, 45] to overcome these limitations, en-
abling precise determination of spectral indices, PBH mass distributions, and SIGW signals.
